topic: A person learns their childhood best friend doesn't remember them
raw_output:
[story 1]

The coffee tasted like ash in my mouth. I stared at the faded photograph, the one from the summer we built forts in Mrs. Henderson’s overgrown backyard. Liam was grinning in it, his arm slung around me, his eyes sparkling with a shared, unspoken history. Now, the silence in the room felt vast, echoing the emptiness where his recognition should have been. I traced the outline of his face with a trembling finger, the paper crinkling under the pressure. Every attempt to bring up a shared memory—the secret handshake, the disastrous attempt at baking a cake—dissolved into a frustrating, hollow sound in my throat. I stood up abruptly, the chair scraping loudly against the hardwood floor, and walked to the window, watching the streetlights blur through the damp evening air. My hands were clenched so tightly my knuckles were white, a physical manifestation of the churning anxiety in my chest. It felt like a fundamental piece of my own narrative had been erased.

[story 2]

"Remember that time?" I asked, my voice sounding brittle, like old glass about to shatter. I watched Liam fiddle with the loose thread on his jacket, his brow furrowed in genuine confusion. He blinked slowly, a deliberate, vacant gesture that felt like a physical slap. "I don't... I don't recall," he finally admitted, the words flat and devoid of inflection. The air seemed to thin around me. I felt a sudden, sharp coldness spread from my core outward, making my skin prickle. I tried again, softer this time, letting a genuine plea seep into my tone. "We used to spend every afternoon there. You always knew exactly what I was thinking." He just looked at me, his gaze distant, as if I were a stranger wearing a familiar face. A tightness constricted my throat, making it difficult to draw a full breath. I backed away slowly, needing distance from the suffocating reality of his unfamiliarity.

[story 3]

I replayed the conversation in my head a hundred times, searching for the misplaced cue, the forgotten context. When Liam laughed at something unrelated, it was a sound that used to vibrate perfectly in sync with mine, a perfect echo. Now, it was just noise, disconnected. I watched him across the crowded room, noticing the subtle shifts in his posture—the way he leaned toward others, the casual ease of his interactions. It was all evidence of a life lived parallel to mine, a life where I was simply an unrecorded footnote. A profound sense of dislocation settled over me, heavy and suffocating. I gripped my glass so hard the crystal threatened to crack. I wanted to scream, to shatter the illusion that this was just a strange lapse in memory, but the effort of vocalizing the pain left me breathless, stuck in a loop of quiet, agonizing disbelief.
